# Mirodan PhD Vol 2 — Chapter III: Externalized Drives (extract)

Source: /Users/opaque/Downloads/Mirodan-PhD-1997-Vol2.pdf, printed pp. 525-557 (PDF pp. 347-379)
Extractor: parallel chapter agent
Status: complete

PDF→printed page offset: PDF page = printed page - 178. Verified at chapter heading (PDF 347 = printed 525) and last page of chapter (PDF 379 = printed 557).

---

## 1. The four Drives — overview

Ch III opens with the canonical list (printed 525):

- DOING (exerting/reacting)
- PASSION (constructing/destroying)
- SPELL (dominating/surrendering)
- VISION (ideas/problems)

Mirodan's gloss: "The four mental states... which are motivated by the subconscious Inner Attitudes and which activate us into the conscious Actions. Each Externalized Drive is revealed (expressed) as a compound of three Elements of movement." (p. 525, quoting "Book" p. 32)

**Key structural facts:**
- Each Drive is a compound of THREE Mental Factors / Motion Factors / Inner Participations / Elements — defined as much by what is "subdued" (the missing fourth) as by what's present (p. 531).
- Inner Attitudes use TWO Motion Factors and appear as Shadow Moves; Drives use THREE and appear as full Working Actions. This is why Drives are "immediately and clearly perceptible" in performance whereas Attitudes are not (p. 530).
- Mental Factor → Drive correspondence (printed 528):
  - SENSING generates DOING
  - FEELING generates PASSION
  - INTUITING generates SPELL
  - THINKING generates VISION

**Carpenter vs. Malmgren tension** (pp. 527-530):
- Carpenter: Inner Attitudes are deeper; Drives are by-products / "transmission belt" between Attitudes and Action. Sequence: subconscious Attitude → Drive → Action.
- Malmgren: reversed. Drives are the "Big Objectives that drive the character in life"; they MOTIVATE Attitudes. Sequence: PSYCHOLOGICAL TYPE → SUPER-OBJECTIVE → EXTERNALIZED DRIVES (INNER) → INNER ATTITUDE → EXTERNALIZED DRIVES (OUTER) → ACTION ATTITUDES (p. 529).
- Mirodan synthesises both as a "red thread running through the character's inner nature as well as through his actions" — a "bridge made of energy" or "spurt of energy transmitting intentions from Super Objective to Character to Action" (p. 530).

Drives have a **dual function**: Inner Attitude is the still "crossroads"; Drives flow IN to feed it, and flow OUT to move character into Action (p. 529).

Note on the "Idea": there is no Character without an Idea (Super-Objective) provoking it. Characters move "towards" or "away from" an Idea at deep emotional level — this is what produces the Drives (p. 528).

---

## 2. Doing

**Definitions (p. 531-532):**
- CHARACTERISTIC: FLOWLESS [Adapting / Feeling subdued]
- MENTAL FACTORS: SENSING THINKING INTUITING [FEELING]
- MOTION FACTORS: WEIGHT SPACE TIME [FLOW]
- INNER PARTICIPATIONS: INTENDING ATTENDING DECIDING [ADAPTING]
- INNER QUESTS: WHAT? WHERE? WHEN? [WHY?]
- ELEMENTS: LIGHT/STRONG, FLEXIBLE/DIRECT, SUSTAINED/QUICK [FREE/BOUND]
- INNER ATTITUDES it can shape: STABLE, NEAR, AWAKE
- Carpenter alt name: "Practical" (Conflict and Harmony Ch. 4)

**Character of Doing:**
- "the dynamic Drive par excellence" — Malmgren likens it to a tank, bulldozer, "steel ball tearing down walls" (p. 532).
- Bayonet-training image: recruits taught to scream as they stab — screaming dominates with sensation the emotions of killing, rendering them "machine-like, full of nothing but blind 'Doing'" (p. 532).
- Defined as **reacting/exerting** (Mirodan: NOT exerting/reacting — she insists on the natural inner-outer order; the soldier reacts to impact first, exerts effort second; one assesses mass/volume of a table before lifting it, p. 532).
- A Doing character "subdues that part of his psychological make-up which is primarily emotional. Thus he moves away from the Attitudes of Mobile, Remote and Adream, which contain Adapting (Feeling)" (p. 533). Not feelingless — but moved away from feeling.
- **Crucial: "We cannot speak, therefore, of a 'Doing' Character, only of a Character whose Inner Attitude is shaped by Doing"** (p. 533). Identification by elimination:
  - Weak Thinking → Awake & Stable degraded → likely Near.
  - Weak Intuiting → Awake & Near degraded → likely Stable.
  - Logical, manipulates ideas under Doing pressure → likely has Awake as Action Attitude.
- The Doing Effort Cube is "very 'square'... no rings or diagonals... looks solid and sensible like the Externalized Drive it represents" (p. 544).

Polonius is the worked example of Doing in the chapter opener (p. 525): "cabinet sessions to chair, ambassadors to receive, players to harangue, intrigues to solve. Faced with a problem... he first 'reacts', then 'exerts' himself. He is, above all, a Doer."

---

## 3. Passion

**Definitions (p. 534):**
- CHARACTERISTIC: SPACELESS [Attending / Thinking subdued]
- MENTAL FACTORS: SENSING [THINKING] INTUITING FEELING
- MOTION FACTORS: WEIGHT [SPACE] TIME FLOW
- INNER PARTICIPATIONS: INTENDING [ATTENDING] DECIDING ADAPTING
- INNER QUESTS: WHAT? [WHERE?] WHY? WHEN?
- ELEMENTS: LIGHT/STRONG, [FLEXIBLE/DIRECT], SUSTAINED/QUICK, FREE/BOUND
- INNER ATTITUDES it can shape: MOBILE, NEAR, ADREAM

**Character of Passion (constructing/destroying):**
- Acts first on the Character's *inner nature*. Begins by "destroying" old, unchallenged decisions in order to "construct" a new understanding (p. 534).
- Worked through the actor's craft itself: the actor "destroys" his own history (place of birth, family, social environment) to "construct" the new Character — painful, like "a refugee who has lost his roots but has not adapted yet to his new country." Talent itself defined as the ability to destroy aspects of inner life to build new ones (p. 534, citing Tapes 29-3).
- Destruction is intimately connected with **change**: "Passion is experienced by Characters and audiences alike as constant change" (p. 535).
- Passion has Feeling but is not exclusively Feeling — it is a compound of Sensing + Feeling + Intuiting. **Above all it is recognizable by its lack of Thinking.** Hence "I've lost my head": Passion overrides Thinking (p. 535).
- Feels (to the Character) as "enthusiastic pleasure," however masochistic (p. 535).
- Often turned destructively against the Character itself (Ophelia is the canonical case).
- In *radiating* Adream characters Passion is "an enthusiasm of emotion"; in *enclosing* Adream or Near characters it is "an enthusiasm of the senses" (p. 535).

Carpenter's Near Passion gloss (p. 535 fn): "a sweating Boiler Attendant overheated emotionally and physically, will slam his boiler door shut with a Passionate Punch as he mutters 'Why... am I doing this ruddy job' demonstrating in his Passionate Action a Thoughtless and Attentionless Feeling of dislike for the boiler."

---

## 4. Spell

**Definitions (p. 536):**
- CHARACTERISTIC: TIMELESS [Deciding / Intuiting subdued — see below for the paradox]
- MENTAL FACTORS: SENSING THINKING [INTUITING] FEELING
- MOTION FACTORS: WEIGHT SPACE [TIME] FLOW
- INNER PARTICIPATIONS: INTENDING ATTENDING [DECIDING] ADAPTING
- INNER QUESTS: WHAT? WHERE? [WHY?] WHEN?
- ELEMENTS: LIGHT/STRONG, FLEXIBLE/DIRECT, [SUSTAINED/QUICK], FREE/BOUND
- INNER ATTITUDES it can shape: STABLE, REMOTE, ADREAM

**Character of Spell (dominating/surrendering):**
- Stanislavsky's "What would I do if I were him?" requires *surrendering* one's own moral/social attitudes to those of the Character. Once surrendered absolutely, the actor (or character) can "dominate" with the Idea — there are no more decisions to take (p. 536).
- Fortinbras in Hamlet: surrenders to circumstances (gathers info, reflects), then dominates the Danish court with the Idea of his absolute right to the crown — Idea of divine right is "timeless," doesn't depend on political expediency. "Almost mystical, Spell-binding quality which awes the Danish nobles into submission" (p. 526).

**The Spell paradox (p. 537), which Mirodan dwells on:**
The Carpenter/Book definition implies Spell is generated by Intuiting and *also* lacks Intuiting — a contradiction. Mirodan lays it out:
> DOING is based on SENSING but has no FEELING
> PASSION is based on FEELING but has no THINKING
> VISION is based on THINKING but has no SENSING
> [paradox] SPELL is based on INTUITING but has no INTUITING

Malmgren's solution: Intuiting is "repressed" to the deepest subconscious in Spell, not absent. The character has stopped taking *new* decisions because it relies on decisions taken long ago (p. 537).

**Mirodan's own emendation (pp. 540-541)** — see §9 below — is to swap the parentage of Spell and Vision so the system aligns with Jung.

**How Spell opposes change (p. 537):**
- *Remote* Spell: "I will die for an idea" — saint or political fanatic; absolute certainty regardless of consequences. Buddha. Great religious figures.
- *Adream* Spell: opposes change because content with achieved position; e.g. Garbo's Queen Christina — "abandoned her own subconscious decision taking in the process of transformation into a royal persona."
- *Stable* Spell: against Mobility, against emotion-driven change; in favour of social/political/personal status quo. Fortinbras is "essentially conservative."

**Tempo signature:** Spell characters generally display a **Sustained tempo**. Because Deciding is rhythmical/time-based, and Deciding is subdued in Spell, the resulting quality is "dream-like: almost, but not literally, in slow motion" (p. 537). Spell has a magnetic quality "akin to that of folk dances or Arabic singing and Gregorian chants — all of which rely on the monotony of repeated gestures to draw participants and audiences alike into their Spell" (p. 537).

**Archetypal link:** Mirodan associates Spell strongly with the Jungian archetypes of the *anima* and the *wise old man* — Stalin, Einstein, Macmillan as historical examples; Garbo for anima projection (pp. 537-538, citing Storr & Jung).

---

## 5. Vision

**Definitions (p. 539):**
- CHARACTERISTIC: WEIGHTLESS [Intending / Sensing subdued]
- MENTAL FACTORS: [SENSING] THINKING INTUITING FEELING
- MOTION FACTORS: [WEIGHT] SPACE TIME FLOW
- INNER PARTICIPATIONS: [INTENDING] ATTENDING DECIDING ADAPTING
- INNER QUESTS: [WHAT?] WHERE? WHEN? WHY?
- ELEMENTS: [LIGHT/STRONG], FLEXIBLE/DIRECT, SUSTAINED/QUICK, FREE/BOUND
- INNER ATTITUDES it can shape: MOBILE, REMOTE, AWAKE

**Character of Vision (ideas/problems):**
- Deals with abstractions, so characters "lose their physical presence — they become 'weightless'" (p. 539).
- "Noiseless, disembodied, floating apparitions": the ghost in Hamlet, Macbeth's vision of Kings, saints in pictorial representations (p. 539).
- BUT Vision need not literally mean lack of physicality (cf. Joan of Arc — rides horses, leads armies; her Vision comes from intuitive intelligence which "counteracts the telluric effect of her Weight and 'lifts' her above her peers and almost above ground" — p. 539 fn).
- The three Visionary Inner Attitudes share **flexibility in thought (Attending)** as common link (p. 539).

**Three Vision sub-flavours (p. 540):**
- *Remote* Vision: Jesus on the cross — "Father forgive them, for they know not what they do" — "ultimate altruistic statement"; surrenders to Idea of forgiveness, dominates persecutors. (Vision×Spell)
- *Mobile* Vision: Ophelia's madness scene — "outpouring of emotion expressed through erratic, flexible associations"; destroys concrete reality and her own identity, constructs idiosyncratic inner world. (Vision×Passion)
- *Awake* Vision: the boffin / scientist "with his head in the clouds, disconnected from practical reality but connected to a world of abstract 'ideas and problems'" — "produces 'projects' — his attitude to ideas is essentially dynamic." (Vision×Doing)

**Vision as omnipresent catalyst** (p. 540): Because every Character requires a Super-Objective ("Big Idea"), Vision is essentially present in *all* Inner Attitudes — even the three Weight-bearing ones (Near, Adream, Stable) which are nominally Visionless. Vision "appears as an omnipresent catalyst," similar to how Time is present in all Attitudes.

---

## 6. Confluence / interaction

**The "Confluence of the Externalized Drives"** is a substantive subsection (printed pp. 546ff.). Each Inner Attitude is born at the meeting of TWO Drives that share exactly one Inner Attitude in common (pp. 546-547):

- STABLE = DOING + SPELL
- MOBILE = PASSION + VISION
- NEAR = DOING + PASSION
- REMOTE = SPELL + VISION
- ADREAM = PASSION + SPELL
- AWAKE = DOING + VISION

Term gloss (p. 546 fn): "the term suggests the confluence of two rivers (the Externalized Drives) joining to create a new, combined flood of energy — the Character, complete with Inner Attitude and Action. Neither the term nor the concept feature in Carpenter's typescript." (i.e. confluence is Malmgren's contribution.)

**Mathematics of confluence:** each Drive subdues one Inner Participation; pairs of Drives subdue two; the remaining two combine into the Inner Attitude (p. 547). Stable arises from Doing+Spell because Mobile (their shared *missing* Attitude) is the combination of the two Inner Participations they BOTH subdue (Adapting + Deciding).

**Inverse mapping (p. 540, restated):**
- Mobile = Vision + Passion = "destroying and constructing with ideas"
- Remote = Vision + Spell = "surrendering to or dominating an idea"
- Awake = Vision + Doing = "reacting to and exerting upon an idea"

**The two Drives in a Character are NOT equal partners (p. 548):** "within it one of the Drives dominates, reflecting the character's psychological type." The dominant Drive corresponds to the Character's *Aspect* — that part of the Inner Attitude which is most developed.

Worked example — Gorky's *Enemies* (p. 548): Zakhar (liberal partner) and Mikhail (boss). Both Near, both Doing+Passion. But Mikhail is Doer-dominant ("Stable to outsiders... but not actually Stable — under stress Passion shows through"), Zakhar is Passion-dominant ("Mobile-looking, but under threat reaches for Doing as nervous agitation"). Same confluence pair, opposite ordering, very different characters.

**Order is interchangeable, governed by Stress (pp. 552, 556-557):**
- "A Near Character can be Doing/Passion at one moment, but Passion/Doing at another."
- The order of Drives is determined by the Stress: e.g. *Time-stressed* Adream is Passion/Spell, *Space-stressed* Adream is Spell/Passion (p. 557).
- Notation: `/` = interchangeable formative pair; `->` = cause/effect from Character to Action. So "Spell/Passion" = formative confluence; "Spell->Vision" = action axis.

**Inner vs Outer Drives (per Action diagram, p. 556):**
- The two formative Drives sit at the *bottom* corners of the X-diagram (Inner Character + Outer Character).
- The two complementary Drives at the *top* corners (Inner Action + Outer Action).
- Adream example: bottom = Passion + Spell; top = Doing + Vision (the Drives that *are absent* from the formative confluence become the Action Drives).
- One top Drive is "extravert" (visible to the audience), the other "introvert" (hidden). Ex: Hawthorne in *The Crucible* — enclosing Stable Time-stressed (Doing+Spell formative), with extravert Passion (his need to assert himself) and introvert Vision (his hidden idea of witches) as Action Drives (p. 556 fn).

---

## 7. Stress patterns / action axes

**Stress is the third dimension** (after Aspect and Inner Attitude) that determines a character's exact location in the system (p. 553). Stress is added to BOTH Inner Participations of an Attitude, not only the Aspect.

For each Inner Attitude there are TWO possible Stresses (the two Motion Factors not used by the Attitude itself):
- Adream (Weight+Flow): Time-stress or Space-stress.
- Etc.

Each Attitude × Aspect (enclosing/radiating) × Stress → 4 diagrams per Attitude × 6 Attitudes = **24 diagrams total** ("'harmonics' produced by the vibrations of the Inner Attitude under the impact of the Stress" — p. 557).

**Action Attitude formation rule (p. 553-554):**
- Outer Action = Stress + Aspect (main Inner Participation).
- Inner Action = Stress + secondary Inner Participation.
- The same Stress acts on both the main and secondary IPs.

Adream worked through (p. 554):
- enclosing + Time-stress → Outer = Near (Diag A); Inner = Mobile (Diag B sec.)
- enclosing + Space-stress → Outer = Stable (Diag B)
- radiating + Time-stress → Outer = Mobile (Diag D)
- radiating + Space-stress → Outer = Remote (Diag C)

**Lining (Mirodan's term, p. 554-555):** When Stress combines with the *residual* (subdued) Inner Participation, it creates a tiny, secondary Action that "lines" the main Action. So a Mobile Outer can carry a Near "lining" (Weight-stressed Mobile) — refining the Action by giving it a subtle additional stress. Twins example (p. 555): Viola = Passion->Vision, Mobile Outer with Near lining (Space-stressed); Sebastian = Passion->Doing, Near Outer with Mobile lining (Weight-stressed Near).

**Action Axis = the diagonal connecting an Inner Drive corner to an Outer Action Drive corner** (pp. 556-557):
- Main Character-Action Axis: Deep-Left → High-Right corner of the X.
- Secondary axis: Deep-Right → High-Left.
- Adream Diag A: Spell/Passion formative, axis Spell->Doing.
- Adream Diag B: Spell/Passion formative, axis Spell->Vision.
- Adream Diag C: Passion/Spell formative, axis Passion->Doing.
- Adream Diag D: Passion/Spell formative, axis Passion->Vision.

**Stress is fluid, not exclusive** (pp. 554, 557 fn): a Stress label means "the main stress occurs more frequently," not that the other Stress is eliminated. Same applies to Variations — "a Character has at its disposal all four Variations given to its Inner Attitude. In practice, we find that one of these variations tends to occur less often, reflecting the character's Aspect... But the other three are used to a large extent and their use makes for variety and interest in performance" (p. 558).

---

## 8. Relation to Inner Attitudes

The two layers are NOT orthogonal — they are *generative*. Every Inner Attitude IS the confluence of two Drives. Reading either way:

- **Drive → Attitude (Carpenter direction):** the actor's Inner Attitudes are the deep psychological substrate; Drives are the visible, conscious "transmission belt" expressing them in action.
- **Drive → Attitude (Malmgren direction):** the Big Idea / Super-Objective generates Drives at the deepest level; Drives in turn create the Inner Attitude.

Mirodan: "an Externalized Drive is the linking force between an internal fusion of Inner Attitudes on the one hand and the Actions of the character on the other. It is a bridge made of energy, or perhaps a spurt of energy transmitting intentions from Super Objective to Character to Action" (p. 530).

**Practical workflow for the actor / analyst (pp. 549-550, 559):**
1. Identify the two formative Drives by elimination (which two Mental Factors are weakest in the character?).
2. The shared Inner Attitude of those two Drives = the Character's Inner Attitude.
3. Determine which Drive is dominant (= Aspect, = psychological type).
4. Determine the Stress (which Action Attitudes does the character favour?).
5. Determine the Action Axis (which Outer Drive shines through).

Examples by play (pp. 561-565):
- *Hamlet*: Gertrude = Passion/Spell, Passion->Doing (Diag C). Claudius = Spell/Passion, Spell->Doing (A). Ophelia = Passion/Spell, Passion->Vision (D). Hamlet himself = Spell/Passion, Spell->Vision (B). Fortinbras = Stable.
- *Streetcar*: Blanche = radiating Time-stressed Adream, Passion->Vision (D). Stella = enclosing Space-stressed Adream, Spell->Doing (A). Stanley = enclosing Time-stressed Adream, Passion->Doing (C).
- *Othello*: Iago, if Near, is Doing/Passion (A) with axis Doing->Spell — "calculating jealousy."

**Critical: Drives are how you *recognize* the Attitude in performance.** Attitudes are subconscious and use only 2 Motion Factors (Shadow Moves) — they aren't directly visible. Drives use 3 Motion Factors and produce full Working Actions, so they are the perceptible track back to the Attitude. "Externalized Drives are used to identify the Inner Attitude of a Character, precisely because this peculiar form of energy is turned outwards and is immediately evident in performance" (p. 527).

---

## 9. Surprises / overrides

**a) The Spell paradox and Mirodan's emendation (pp. 537, 540-541)**

Carpenter's strict definition makes Spell both generated by Intuiting AND lacking Intuiting — Malmgren himself "confesses to be puzzled by this part of Carpenter's book" (p. 537). Mirodan reads Carpenter's later typescript (*Conflict and Harmony* Ch. 3) more closely and finds Carpenter himself drifting: there Spell is "Decisionless but full of THOUGHT (thoughtful)" and Vision is "Weightless but full of INTUITION." Mirodan therefore *proposes amending the system* (p. 541):
> SPELL is generated by THINKING and lacks INTUITING (is Timeless)
> VISION is generated by INTUITING and lacks SENSING (is Weightless)

This restores Jung's original Intuition–Sensation opposition and makes "creative thinking" a function of Intuition (lateral thinking, "seeing things in an unexpected light" — Wittgenstein cited as a Spell exemplar of "cold, logical Spell" generated by systematic thought).

This is a substantial subversion of the simpler Mental-Factor → Drive table given on p. 528. The reader of Mirodan needs to know whether the working scheme is Carpenter-canonical or Mirodan-amended.

**b) "There is no pure Doing/Passion/Spell/Vision character"**

Mirodan repeatedly insists you cannot speak of "a Doing Character" — only of a Character "shaped by" Doing (p. 533). Drives are tendencies, not types.

**c) The diagrams show all four Mental Factors are always present** (p. 557):

> "A Character uses all its four Mental Factors and Inner Participations. Attitudes are not... a matter of excluding altogether any of these functions: even the ones which do not feature within the Inner Attitude are there, residually, in the Action Attitudes."

So "subdued" never means absent. The three-Drive Mental Factor compound is a matter of *proportion and emphasis*, not exclusion. This subverts any simple "type = Drive A + Drive B" reading.

**d) Inner/Outer Drive ordering flips in real time** (p. 552 fn):

> "A Near Character can be Doing/Passion at one moment, but Passion/Doing at another. This does not invalidate the idea that the Inner Character is a function of the psychological type (Aspect) of the Character. It simply means that sometimes the immediate need to act elicited by the given circumstances of a scene can prevail and that the Externalized Drive associated with the Outer Character becomes preponderant."

The order of formative Drives is *not stable across a scene* — it tracks Stress, which tracks circumstance.

**e) "Lining" complicates Outer Action reads**

A Mobile Outer Action is not pure Mobile if it carries a Near lining (Weight-stress) — sensuous quality colours the emotional display. Two characters with the same nominal Outer Action can read very differently because of opposite linings (Viola vs Sebastian, p. 555 fn).

**f) Vision is everywhere even when "absent"** (p. 540):

Because every Character has a Super-Objective and the Super-Objective is "essentially visionary," Vision must be present (catalytically) in all six Attitudes — including the three nominally Weight-bearing (Visionless) ones (Near, Adream, Stable). Vision is "an omnipresent catalyst, similar in its effect to the Motion Factor of Time, which... is present in all Attitudes."

**g) The whole system is in motion, not static** (p. 558, closing of Ch III):

> "The Inner Attitude, its components, its satellite Attitudes and the 'rockets' of Externalized Drives flying between them are all in perpetual motion. The skill of the actor or director is to use the terms of the system in order to chart the precise location of a given moment in the life of the Character on the map of this constellation."

Reading a character is a dynamic, frame-by-frame act of triangulation, not a static label.

---

## 10. Page reference index

Printed page (PDF page) — content:

- 525 (347) — Ch III opener / four Drives list
- 525-527 (347-349) — Polonius (Doing), Ophelia (Passion), Fortinbras (Spell), Hamlet (Vision) introduced
- 527-528 (349-350) — definitional difficulties; Carpenter glossary
- 528-529 (350-351) — Malmgren's reversal; Mental-Factor → Drive table; full sequence diagram
- 530 (352) — Mirodan's "red thread" synthesis; Drives as 3 Motion Factors vs Attitudes as 2 (Shadow Moves)
- 531 (353) — "subdued" element defines a Drive; three-element rule
- 531-533 (353-355) — DOING entry with cube
- 533 (355) — bayonet / bulldozer images; reacting/exerting reorder
- 534-535 (356-357) — PASSION entry with cube
- 535 (357) — "I've lost my head"; radiating vs enclosing Passion
- 536-537 (358-359) — SPELL entry with cube; the Spell paradox
- 537 (359) — Spell sub-flavours by Inner Attitude (Remote, Adream, Stable)
- 537-538 (359-360) — Spell tempo (Sustained); folk dance / Gregorian chant analogy; anima/wise old man archetypes
- 539 (361) — VISION entry with cube
- 540 (362) — three Visionary sub-flavours (Remote/Mobile/Awake); Vision as omnipresent catalyst
- 540-541 (362-363) — Mirodan's PROPOSED EMENDATION (Spell ↔ Vision parentage)
- 542 (364) — Vision cube; closing of Drive definitions
- 543-545 (365-367) — construction of the Drive Cubes (mostly graphical)
- 546-548 (368-370) — Confluence: math; six Drive-pair → Attitude mappings; Gorky *Enemies* example
- 548-557 (370-379) — the 24 Action Diagrams; Inner/Outer Character; Stress; Action Axis; lining; extravert/introvert Action Drives; "harmonics" closer
